channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: U.S. Intelligence Shows Iran Retains Substantial Missile Capabilities
item.description: Secret new assessments say Iran has operational access to 30 of its 33 missile sites along the Strait of Hormuz, suggesting that its military remains far stronger than President Trump has asserted.
item.dc:creator: Adam Entous, Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Missile displays in Tehran in 2024. According to classified U.S. intelligence assessments, Iran retains roughly 70 percent of its prewar missile stockpile.

item.title: Battle Over the Strait of Hormuz Leaves Safe Passage a Gamble
item.description: Vessels are traveling without transmitting critical information meant to keep the waters safe, maritime intelligence experts say.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni and Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Ships in the Strait of Hormuz near Oman on Friday.

item.title: Trump’s Shrinking Ambitions on China
item.description: The president came into office planning harsher trade moves on China than on the rest of the world. Here’s why he’s had to scale them back.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.media:description: When President Trump tried to force China into making concessions last year by threatening extreme tariffs, the tactic backfired, forcing the United States to pare back its goals.

item.title: The Lecturer and Philosopher King: Xi Jinping Behind Closed Doors
item.description: Encounters with other world leaders reveal a side of China’s leader that the public rarely sees, and offer clues to how he will approach President Trump in Beijing.
item.dc:creator: David Pierson
item.media:description: Xi Jinping, China’s leader, at the National People’s Congress in Beijing in March.

item.title: How China Could Wield Its Control of Rare Earths Against Trump
item.description: A central question hanging over the summit this week is whether China will agree to extend a temporary postponement of even tougher rare-earth export controls.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher
item.media:description: Steel mills, chemical factories and rare-earth refineries in Baotou, China. The Trump administration said it was in frequent communication with China about rare earths.

item.title: Will Trump and Xi Try to Slow the A.I. Arms Race?
item.description: The leaders of both countries are expected to discuss the risks from artificial intelligence, but neither country is willing to be the first to slow down.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Wang
item.media:description: President Trump and President Xi Jinping meeting in Busan, South Korea, in 2025.

item.title: Kash Patel Denies Lying and Excessive Drinking in Heated Exchanges With Democrats
item.description: The remarks, at a hearing ostensibly about the coming budget for law enforcement agencies, veered from sedate exchanges about operational matters to ugly personal confrontations.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Kash Patel, the director of the F.B.I., sparred with Democratic lawmakers on Tuesday on Capitol Hill.

item.title: Justice Dept. Officials Consider Settling Trump Suit Against I.R.S.
item.description: One of the settlement terms under review is for the I.R.S. to drop any audits of the president, his family members and businesses.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren and Alan Feuer
item.media:description: President Trump sued the Internal Revenue Service in January over the leak of his tax returns during his first term.

item.title: Trump’s ‘Golden Dome’ Missile Defense Plan Could Cost $1.2 Trillion
item.description: A report from the Congressional Budget Office said that space-based interceptors, which do not currently exist, would probably consume 60 percent of the total cost.
item.dc:creator: John Ismay
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: President Trump during an announcement on his plan to develop the “Golden Dome” missile defense program at the White House last May.

item.title: Why Your Life Is Now on Subscriptions
item.description: As companies look to build cash flow and loyalty, everything from heated car seats to earthworm deliveries can become a recurring charge on your credit card.
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.media:description: From streaming services to car features, more companies are moving toward a model that turns customers into lifelong subscribers.

item.title: Cindy Burbank Wins Senate Primary in Nebraska, but Plans to Drop Out to Help Dan Osborn
item.description: Democrats in Nebraska appear headed toward the Senate matchup they want, while their primary for a key House district was too close to call.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Walker Pickering for The New York Times
item.media:description: Cindy Burbank, pictured during a recent motorcycle ride, won the Democratic nomination for the U.S. Senate in Nebraska, but she said she planned to drop out of the race in an effort to help Dan Osborn, an independent, win instead.

item.title: As Becerra Rises in Polls, He Draws Fire for Trying to Direct Interview
item.description: “This is not a ‘gotcha’ piece, right?” the leading Democratic candidate for California governor asked a television reporter before an interview.
item.dc:creator: Orlando Mayorquín
item.media:description: Xavier Becerra, the health secretary in the Biden administration, is now the leading Democratic candidate in polls on the California governor’s race.

item.title: Nebraska Gov. Jim Pillen to Face Democrat Lynne Walz
item.description: Democrats face an uphill fight as they seek to unseat Gov. Jim Pillen, who is running for another term in the solidly Republican state.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith
item.media:description: Lynne Walz, a Democrat and former state legislator, is running for governor of Nebraska.

item.title: Jen Kiggans, Virginia Republican, Faces Uproar Over Radio Host’s Racist Remark About Jeffries
item.description: Representative Jen Kiggans, a Republican in a tough re-election fight, faced criticism after she agreed with comments by a local radio host that included a racist line about Hakeem Jeffries, the Democratic minority leader.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein and Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Jen Kiggans of Virginia, a Republican, said on Monday night that her remarks had been misconstrued by Democrats.

item.title: Britain Has Had 5 Leaders in a Decade. Is It About to Get Another?
item.description: The promise of Keir Starmer’s Labour Party in 2024 was renewed stability — but that now appears to be under threat.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:description: Boris Johnson, left, Liz Truss and David Cameron

item.title: Europe Tries a Trumpian Tactic With Trump: No Apologies
item.description: Stuck with the fallout from America’s war in Iran, European leaders have criticized the president publicly. When he’s been angered, they haven’t backed down.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Chancellor Friedrich Merz of Germany is one of several European leaders who has openly criticized President Trump.

item.title: Catherine, Princess of Wales, to Visit Italy
item.description: Catherine will visit Reggio Emilia, an Italian city celebrated for its approach to early childhood education.
item.dc:creator: Elisabetta Povoledo and Amelia Nierenberg
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:description: Catherine, Princess of Wales, in London last month.

item.title: Trump Administration to Tap Longtime ICE Official to Lead Agency
item.description: David Venturella’s appointment is in line with a push by Markwayne Mullin, the homeland security secretary, to keep his department under the radar and out of headlines.
item.dc:creator: Hamed Aleaziz and Tyler Pager
item.media:credit: Jenna Schoenefeld for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Trump administration will install David Venturella, a former career agency official who worked at a private prison company, as acting head of Immigration and Customs Enforcement.

item.title: Dominican Republic Agrees to Take Third-Country Migrants Deported by U.S.
item.description: The agreement, which reverses President Luis Abinader’s previous stance, is part of President Trump’s efforts to find governments willing to accept deportees.
item.dc:creator: James Wagner and Hogla Enecia Pérez
item.media:description: President Luis Abinader of the Dominican Republic previously said, “We do not have, nor will we accept, nor are we obligated to, nor will we accept receiving people from other countries.”

item.title: A Dying Son, His Detained Parents and a Race to Reunite Before the End
item.description: From his hospital bed in Chicago, Kevin Gonzalez had a final wish to see his parents. But they were in immigration detention in Arizona after illegally crossing to see their son.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy

item.title: Florida Plans to Close ‘Alligator Alcatraz,’ Vendors Are Reportedly Told
item.description: The Department of Homeland Security has determined the state-run immigration detention facility is too expensive, and some private vendors have struggled to front costs.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei and Hamed Aleaziz
item.media:description: Florida intends to shut down a high-profile immigration detention center that opened last summer in the Everglades.

item.title: Jason Collins, First Active N.B.A. Player to Come Out as Gay, Dies at 47
item.description: His achievements on the court were eclipsed by an essay he wrote in Sports Illustrated in 2013 in which he declared: “I’m a 34-year-old N.B.A. center. I’m Black and I’m gay.”
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.category: Same-Sex Marriage, Civil Unions and Domestic Partnerships
item.media:description: Jason Collins in 2014, the year after he announced he was gay in a front-page essay in Sports Illustrated magazine.

item.title: How an ‘Impossible’ Idea Led to a Pancreatic Cancer Breakthrough
item.description: The new strategy also holds promise for lung and colon tumors. Here’s how scientists discovered it.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata and Rebecca Robbins
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times

item.title: P.C.O.S. Has Been Renamed P.M.O.S. Doctors Hope It Will Improve Care for Millions.
item.description: The condition has been renamed polyendocrine metabolic ovarian syndrome, or P.M.O.S., to better reflect its wide-ranging health effects.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Astor

item.title: Israeli Report Finds That Sexual Violence by Hamas Was Widespread
item.description: A two-year investigation by a team of researchers in Israel concluded that sexual violence by Hamas and its allies was widespread during and after the Oct. 7, 2023, attack.
item.dc:creator: Isabel Kershner
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:credit: Avishag Shaar-Yashuv for The New York Times
item.media:description: A house in kibbutz Kfar Aza, Israel, that was among those attacked on Oct. 7, 2023.

item.title: Hantavirus Outbreak Is Resurrecting Covid-Era Misinformation Tactics
item.description: Experts say A.I. tools have made it even easier for influencers and others to spread false messages online.
item.dc:creator: Teddy Rosenbluth and Steven Lee Myers

item.title: How a Hantavirus Cruise Passenger Is Spending His Quarantine in Nebraska
item.description: Jake Rosmarin, a travel influencer from Boston, is one of 15 passengers from the virus-stricken cruise ship who are being monitored at the National Quarantine Unit in Nebraska.
item.dc:creator: Sonia A. Rao

item.title: Should You Worry About Hantavirus?
item.description: Here’s what to know about the virus, how it spreads and the risk to the general public.
item.dc:creator: Nina Agrawal and Dani Blum

item.title: Our Favorite Moments From This Year’s Tony-Nominated Broadway Shows
item.description: Here are some of the brilliant moments our writers can’t shake from this year’s batch of Tony-nominated productions.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw, Jesse Green, Laura Collins-Hughes, Elisabeth Vincentelli and Sara Krulwich
item.category: Two Strangers (Carry a Cake Across New York) (Play)

item.title: Xi Jinping Is Planning for China’s Final Victory Over the U.S.
item.description: While Trump chases quick wins and flashy optics, Xi plays a generational game for global dominance.
item.dc:creator: Julian Gewirtz
item.media:credit: Illustration by The New York Times

item.title: NATO, Please Help. Trump Has No Strategy for Iran.
item.description: NATO should overcome its distaste for President Trump and sends its navies to help open up the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Thomas L. Friedman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Jordan Klepper Wants the President to Get More Sleep
item.description: “Well, well, well, looks like the Sleepy Joe-er has become the sleepy Joe-ee,” Klepper said after President Trump was seen with his eyes closed during an event in the Oval Office.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: U.S. and Mexican Officials Deny C.I.A. Had Lethal Role in Mexico Operation
item.description: The denials came after a CNN report claimed the C.I.A. had been involved in the assassination of a cartel member inside Mexico earlier this year.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes and Maria Abi-Habib
item.media:credit: Carl De Souza/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: “The Government of Mexico categorically rejects any version that seeks to normalize, justify, or suggest the existence of lethal, covert, or unilateral operations by foreign agencies on national territory,” said Omar García Harfuch, Mexico’s security secretary

item.title: In San Francisco, the Tents of Homeless People Are Disappearing
item.description: Mayor Daniel Lurie and city leaders have enforced bans on camping and moved people off sidewalks to reduce visible homelessness. Critics say too many homeless people are housed in jail.
item.dc:creator: Heather Knight
item.media:credit: Mike Kai Chen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mayor Daniel Lurie of San Francisco spoke with a city worker in 2025 about cleaning up the Tenderloin neighborhood.
